__fixture__.name: restore_creates_new_checkpoint
__fixture__.scenario: The user opens a restore preview on an older entry (lh-2026041911-000042 from the typing_edit_save_checkpoint fixture) for a file that has since been renamed. The preview carries every field in restore_preview_required_fields: the source entry, the last-known canonical identity, the current canonical identity, canonical_identity_drift = rename_detected, the rename/move chain, body availability, resulting_snapshot_class = restore_rollback_checkpoint, and the id of the new checkpoint the restore will write on apply. On apply, the lane writes this record: snapshot_class = restore_rollback_checkpoint, actor_class = restore_rollback_runner, reversal_class = restore_from_checkpoint, restored_from_entry_id citing the source. The source entry is left untouched — the prior trail is never invisibly rewritten.
doc_sections: §4 Logical document identity and rename / move
§8 Restore as a new attributable checkpoint

rename_move_history.note: Rename via refactor_engine before the restore preview.

capture_descriptor.omission_note: Restore re-addressed the original content-hashed blob from lh-2026041911-000042.

restore_preview.note: Preview quoted the rename chain verbatim before apply.

side_effect_summary: Restore created a new attributable checkpoint at the renamed path; the source entry remains on the timeline.
notes: Satisfies the acceptance rule: 'Restoring from local history creates a new attributable checkpoint instead of invisibly rewriting the prior trail.'
